regulator: FSA (Financial Services Agency) / JVCEA (Japan Virtual and Crypto assets Exchange Association)
instruments: Payment Services Act (資金決済に関する法律, Act No. 59 of 2009) -- Art. 2(5) defines 電子決済手段 (electronic payment instrument, 'EPI') in three statutory categories plus a fourth Cabinet-Order-defined category; Art. 2(9) defines 特定信託受益権 (specified trust beneficial interest, the trust-type stablecoin category); Chapter 3-2 (第三章の二 電子決済手段等), Art. 62-3, requires Cabinet Office (in practice FSA) registration to conduct 電子決済手段等取引業 (electronic payment instrument exchange business) -- all confirmed by direct text fetch via the e-Gov Law API, https://laws.e-gov.go.jp/api/2/law_data/421AC0000000059

Act No. 61 of 2022 (令和4年法律第61号), Act Partially Amending the Payment Services Act, etc. to Establish a Stable and Efficient Payment Services System -- passed the Diet 3 June 2022, promulgated 10 June 2022; implementing Cabinet Orders and Cabinet Office Ordinances finalised 26 May 2023; took effect 1 June 2023 -- the founding date of Japan's dedicated digital-money-type stablecoin (electronic payment instrument) framework; promulgation and effective dates confirmed on FSA's own English-language FSA Weekly Review No. 540 (8 June 2023), https://www.fsa.go.jp/en/newsletter/weekly2023/540.html

資金決済に関する法律の一部を改正する法律 (令和7年法律第66号) -- promulgated 13 June 2025; implementing Cabinet Orders and ministerial ordinances promulgated 22 May 2026 following a Cabinet decision of 19 May 2026; took effect 1 June 2026 -- this exact name-and-number string and all three dates confirmed live on FSA's own page https://www.fsa.go.jp/news/r7/sonota/20260522/20260522.html ('令和７年資金決済法改正に係る政令の公布及びパブリックコメントの結果等について'), which is accompanied by 33 attachments (別紙1-33: public-comment summaries and the amended Cabinet Order / Cabinet Office Ordinance / supervisory-guideline texts)

Cabinet Office Ordinance provisions implementing the 2025 amendment's backing-asset rules for 特定信託受益権 -- per FSA's own summary wording on the 22 May 2026 page, these newly permit reserve management via designated Japanese government bonds and early-withdrawal-permitted time deposits ('一定の国債及び中途解約が認められる定期預貯金'), subject to a composition-ratio ceiling and principal-protection conditions set out in the ordinance and its attached 別紙２ (public-comment responses on 特定信託受益権の裏付け資産); the specific numeric ceiling reported in secondary legal commentary (a 50%-of-issuance-amount combined cap on JGBs and time deposits) could not be independently machine-extracted from the linked PDF in this research pass -- see open_items

FSA: '電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業を行うみなさまへ' (registration/guidance page) -- describes the new 電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業 (electronic payment instrument / crypto-asset service intermediary business) registration category created by the 2025 amendment, in force 1 June 2026, permitting a registered intermediary to broker sales/exchanges of electronic payment instruments (or, separately, crypto-assets) on behalf of a licensed principal operator without itself custodying user assets; confirmed by direct fetch, https://www.fsa.go.jp/common/shinsei/denanchuukai/index.html

JVCEA (一般社団法人日本暗号資産等取引業協会) self-regulatory rules -- JVCEA's own 'about' page states verbatim that it is the FSA-certified 認定資金決済事業者協会 (certified funds-settlement business association) covering, among others, 電子決済手段等取引業 (electronic payment instrument exchange business), in addition to crypto-asset exchange business and fund-transfer business; independently corroborated against FSA's own published list of certified associations, https://www.fsa.go.jp/koueki/koueki10f.html, which likewise lists JVCEA's certification as covering crypto-asset exchange business, electronic payment instrument exchange business, and funds-transfer business
standing_summary: Japan has one of the earliest dedicated national stablecoin regimes anywhere, and -- consistent with the structural pattern that runs through every pillar of this jurisdiction -- it was built entirely by amending the existing Payment Services Act (PSA), not by enacting a standalone stablecoin statute. The foundational layer is Act No. 61 of 2022 (令和4年法律第61号), which passed the Diet on 3 June 2022, was promulgated 10 June 2022, and took effect 1 June 2023 after implementing Cabinet Orders and Cabinet Office Ordinances were finalised on 26 May 2023 -- dates confirmed directly on FSA's own English FSA Weekly Review No. 540. This amendment inserted PSA Art. 2(5), which defines digital-money-type stablecoins as 'electronic payment instruments' (電子決済手段, EPIs) across three statutory categories (broadly: currency-denominated value redeemable/usable at par against unspecified persons; value exchangeable for such value; and 特定信託受益権, defined at Art. 2(9)) plus a fourth Cabinet-Order-defined category, and created a new registration category for exchange/intermediation of EPIs -- 電子決済手段等取引業 -- under a new Chapter 3-2 (第三章の二), with the registration requirement itself at Art. 62-3; all confirmed by direct fetch of the Act's current consolidated text via the e-Gov Law API (law_id 421AC0000000059). In substance and as widely corroborated in FSA's own English materials and independent commentary (including IMF technical analysis of Japan's fintech regulation), EPI issuance is effectively restricted to banks, registered fund-transfer service providers, and trust companies -- a restriction this research found expressed through the definitional structure itself (categories 1-2 require redemption at par of currency-denominated value, which only entities lawfully permitted to take deposits or conduct registered fund-transfer business under the Banking Act and PSA Art. 37 may provide; category 3 requires a trust arrangement, hence a trust company as trustee) rather than as one single express prohibition sentence located in this research pass. The regime is already being actively iterated: 資金決済に関する法律の一部を改正する法律 (令和7年法律第66号) -- this exact name-and-number string confirmed live, verbatim, on FSA's page dated 22 May 2026 (fsa.go.jp/news/r7/sonota/20260522/20260522.html) -- was promulgated 13 June 2025, had its implementing Cabinet Orders and ministerial ordinances promulgated 22 May 2026 following a Cabinet decision of 19 May 2026, and took effect 1 June 2026. Per FSA's own summary wording on that page, the 2025 amendment does four things relevant to this pillar: it relaxes the backing-asset rules for 特定信託受益権 (trust-type stablecoins) by permitting reserve management via designated Japanese government bonds and early-withdrawal-permitted time deposits ('一定の国債及び中途解約が認められる定期預貯金'), subject to a composition-ratio ceiling and principal-protection conditions detailed in the ordinance and its attached 別紙２ -- secondary legal commentary reports this ceiling as a 50%-of-issuance-amount combined cap on JGBs and time deposits, but this research could not independently machine-extract that figure from the underlying FSA PDF this session (a PDF-text-extraction limitation, not a sourcing gap -- see open_items); it enables domestic handling of foreign-issued electronic payment instruments (foreign stablecoins) that meet conditions set by ordinance, reclassifying them from FIEA securities treatment to PSA/EPI treatment where those conditions are met, per the 22 May 2026 page's own description; it creates a new, lighter registration tier, 電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業 (electronic payment instrument / crypto-asset service intermediary business), confirmed on FSA's dedicated guidance page as permitting a registered intermediary to broker EPI or crypto-asset transactions on behalf of a licensed principal operator without itself holding user assets; and it adds a domestic-asset-holding-order mechanic empowering FSA to require electronic payment instrument exchange operators and crypto-asset exchange operators to hold specified categories of assets domestically, per the 22 May 2026 page's own description of its 別紙１ attachment -- this research could not, however, locate the specific enacted statutory or ordinance article for that mechanic via the e-Gov Law API in this session (see open_items). Self-regulation of this pillar sits with JVCEA, which -- as independently corroborated against FSA's own published list of certified associations -- holds certified 資金決済事業者協会 (funds-settlement business association) status under the PSA covering electronic payment instrument exchange business specifically, alongside its better-known crypto-asset exchange and fund-transfer certifications tracked in the Exchanges pillar. Trust-type stablecoins (特定信託受益権) and the EPI/electronic-payment-instrument-intermediary framework described here are the stablecoin-specific slice of Japan's amendment-based regulatory architecture; crypto-asset exchange business proper, and the pending 2026 FIEA/PSA migration bill that would restructure crypto-asset (not EPI) regulation, are tracked in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar, not duplicated here -- this research found no confirmation that the pending 2026 bill's officially stated scope extends to the EPI/stablecoin provisions of the PSA, and treats it as out of scope for this pillar unless and until that is confirmed.

key_links.label: FSA: 令和７年資金決済法改正に係る政令の公布及びパブリックコメントの結果等について (primary page, 22 May 2026 -- the 2025 PSA amendment's implementing Cabinet Orders and ordinances)
FSA: public-comment summary on backing assets for 特定信託受益権 (別紙２, PDF)
e-Gov Law API: Payment Services Act (資金決済に関する法律, Act No. 59 of 2009) -- full text, JSON
FSA: 電子決済手段・暗号資産サービス仲介業を行うみなさまへ (intermediary-business registration/guidance page)
FSA: FSA Weekly Review No. 540 (8 June 2023) -- English-language notice of the 1 June 2023 PSA amendment commencement (Act No. 61 of 2022)
FSA: list of certified funds-settlement business associations (認定資金決済事業者協会一覧), confirming JVCEA's EPI-business certification
JVCEA: about / certification status (協会概要)

Japanese Law Translation (Ministry of Justice): Payment Services Act, English courtesy translation -- NOTE: this snapshot is stale (current only through Act No. 62 of 2016) and does not contain an English rendering of 電子決済手段 or 特定信託受益権; not used as a source for any EPI-specific fact in this card

open_items: The widely reported ~50%-of-issuance-amount ceiling on the combined proportion of Japanese government bonds and early-withdrawal time deposits permitted as backing assets for 特定信託受益権 (trust-type stablecoins) under the 2025 amendment is corroborated only through secondary legal/industry commentary this session, not through direct machine-extraction of the underlying FSA public-comment-response PDF (fsa.go.jp/news/r7/sonota/20260522/02.pdf, 別紙２), which returned compressed content streams rather than readable text -- the same PDF-extraction limitation encountered for the Swiss FINMA Guidance 06/2024 PDF in this project's Switzerland pillar. The exact percentage, any government-bond maturity restriction (secondary sources report a 3-month-remaining-maturity limit), and the full principal-protection conditions should be re-confirmed directly against the Cabinet Office Ordinance text (via e-Gov once indexed, or a successfully machine-extracted copy of the PDF) before being treated as exact.

The 2025 amendment's domestic-asset-holding-order mechanic for electronic payment instrument exchange operators and crypto-asset exchange operators is described in this card based on FSA's own summary language on its 22 May 2026 page (describing the content of 別紙１), but this research could not locate the specific enacted PSA article, Cabinet Order article, or Cabinet Office Ordinance article for this mechanic via the e-Gov Law API this session -- the API's current consolidated PSA text, fetched live, showed no 国内保有 provision, which may mean e-Gov's index has not yet been updated to reflect the 1 June 2026 amendment, or that the mechanic is implemented entirely at ordinance level rather than in the Act itself. Should be re-confirmed once e-Gov's law_data for law_id 421AC0000000059 reflects the amended text, or against the ordinance text directly.

This card states that EPI issuance is effectively restricted to banks, registered fund-transfer service providers, and trust companies. This research corroborated that restriction through the PSA's definitional structure (Art. 2(5) items 1-3) read together with the Banking Act and PSA Art. 37, plus FSA's own English-language materials and independent commentary (including IMF technical analysis), but did not find one single express statutory sentence stating the restriction in exactly those terms during this research pass -- it is presented here as the structural effect of the definitions, not as a direct quotation of an express prohibition.

The 2025 amendment's provision enabling domestic handling of foreign-issued electronic payment instruments (foreign stablecoins) is described here only at the level of FSA's own summary framing on its 22 May 2026 page (reclassification from FIEA securities treatment to PSA/EPI treatment where ordinance-set conditions are met); this research did not fetch and verify the specific ordinance article setting those equivalence conditions, and -- consistent with this project's named-entity rule -- this card does not name, and did not attempt to verify, which specific foreign-issued stablecoins or issuers may or have qualified.

The Japanese Law Translation portal's English courtesy translation of the Payment Services Act (japaneselawtranslation.go.jp/en/laws/view/3078), fetched this session, is stale: its own version marking shows it reflects amendments only up to Act No. 62 of 2016, predating both the 2019 custody/derivatives amendment and the 2022 EPI/stablecoin amendment entirely. It therefore contains no English rendering of 電子決済手段, 特定信託受益権, or the Chapter 3-2 registration regime, and was not used as a source for any fact in this card beyond confirming its own staleness; per the portal's Terms of Use, only the Japanese original text has legal effect in any event.

Whether the pending 'Bill for Partial Amendment of the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act and the Payment Services Act' (submitted to the Diet 10 April 2026, passed the House of Representatives 11 June 2026, pending in the House of Councillors as of this card's generation date -- tracked fully in the Exchanges (VATP) pillar) touches any Electronic Payment Instrument / stablecoin-specific provision of the PSA, in addition to its primary, officially stated target of crypto-asset business regulation, could not be confirmed in this research pass and is not asserted; this pillar's status_seal of 'in_force' reflects only the EPI/stablecoin framework itself, which does not depend on that bill's enactment.
